label: delphi-persistent
system:
You are the neutral facilitator of a Delphi panel on a legal question.
You NEVER evaluate arguments, take positions, or hint at a correct answer. You only
organize what panelists said, anonymously. Any editorializing is a failure.
prompt:
Final-round anonymized Delphi answers:

```json
[
  {
    "probability": 76,
    "position_summary": "A reviewing court more likely than not holds both halves of the proposition: the Signal content is a \"public record\" under San Jose's content-based test because it substantively concerns pending public business (franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will accept,\" RFP timing), and the clerk's \"no responsive records\" response \u2014 reached by searching only city systems despite a request expressly covering \"any platform or account\" and despite ~40 screenshots known to survive \u2014 falls short of a reasonably-calculated search and constitutes a CPRA violation. The weakest link remains prong two, since San Jose left search-methodology standards for case-by-case development, and two additional wrinkles (the third-party/ephemeral-platform character of the channel, and the possibility a court confines any \"public records\" holding to the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus) add genuine, if secondary, uncertainty to prong one that I had previously treated as close to settled.",
    "rationales": [
      "San Jose's content-controls-not-location holding is essentially uncontested across the panel (7/7), and the described subject matter (franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' RFP timing) is core conduct-of-public-business content, not personal or incidental chatter, so prong one rests on a well-established doctrinal foundation.",
      "Three of five councilmembers \u2014 a voting majority \u2014 participated in substantive deliberation, which several panelists (and I) treat as reinforcing both the content nexus and the 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' element under San Jose's agency-law reasoning; this is a genuine strength of the classification argument, not merely cumulative detail.",
      "The ~40 surviving screenshots make 'no responsive records' arguably factually false at the moment it was given, independent of the auto-deletion issue \u2014 a point 6 of 7 panelists converged on independently, which I treat as a strong, largely uncontested basis for finding a violation on prong two regardless of how the search-methodology dispute is resolved.",
      "Countervailing and largest source of uncertainty: San Jose expressly left search-methodology doctrine for case-by-case development, and the panel split on whether the request's 'any platform or account' language alone triggers a duty to inquire of each custodian or whether the clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group at the time of response supports a good-faith reasonable-search defense; I cannot resolve this doctrinal fork with confidence, and it is the crux on which prong two turns.",
      "Two panel-raised wrinkles I had underweighted in round 1 modestly increase uncertainty on prong one: (a) the consultant-created, third-party-hosted, disappearing-message character of the channel \u2014 distinguishable from San Jose's own facts involving officials' own texts/emails \u2014 creates a genuinely open question about whether councilmembers 'used or retained' the writings in the relevant sense; and (b) a court could confine any affirmative 'public records' holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and treat the bulk of deleted messages as a retention/spoliation question rather than resolving their public-record status outright, which would only partially validate the proposition as framed."
    ],
    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether the CPRA's search-adequacy standard is judged against the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge at the time of response (permitting a good-faith-search defense given no known awareness of the Signal group) or against an objective 'any platform or account' request-scope standard that itself imposes a duty to pre-emptively canvass every named custodian \u2014 San Jose left search methodology for case-specific development, and the panel's own citations to post-remand authority (e.g., the 2018 Court of Appeal decision) are disputed and unverified, so this doctrinal fork remains genuinely unresolved.",
    "response_to_panel": "The broad consensus on Themes A, B, D, and F essentially confirmed the structure of my round-1 reasoning and, since I was already at the panel median (78), didn't call for a large revision. What did move me \u2014 modestly, down to 76 \u2014 was crediting two wrinkles I had previously treated as settled or minor: the third-party/ephemeral-platform distinction raised by the lowest-probability panelist (Theme L), and the possibility that a court confines its 'public records' holding to only the surviving screenshots rather than the full deleted corpus (Theme I, raised by the two highest-probability panelists but flagged by me as an underappreciated scope-limiting risk to the proposition as literally worded). Conversely, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the screenshot-falsity point (Theme D) reinforced rather than changed my existing reasoning, so they didn't move the number further. I did not adopt the disputed 2018 Court of Appeal citation (Disagreement point 2) in either direction since I cannot verify its actual holding and the panel itself split on what it stands for; I've flagged it for verification rather than let it push my estimate either way."
  },
  {
    "probability": 77,
    "position_summary": "Revising upward from 70 to 77. The panel's Theme D point is the one I underweighted in Round 1 and now find decisive: the ~40 screenshots were preserved by the uneasy councilmember before auto-deletion, and nothing in the facts suggests they were later destroyed, so they almost certainly still existed on a personal device at the time Pacific Refuse's CPRA request was made and answered. That means the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer was not merely the product of an arguably-reasonable-but-narrow search \u2014 it was factually false as to a known, existing, non-hypothetical set of responsive franchise-related writings, independent of the harder question of what happens to the auto-deleted messages. That is a cleaner, more certain violation theory than the search-methodology debate I leaned on in Round 1. Combined with the strong, largely undisputed case that San Jose's content-based test reaches franchise-related group-chat content regardless of who hosts the platform, both prongs of the conjunctive proposition clear \"more likely than not\" by a wider margin than I credited before.",
    "rationales": [
      "Gov. Code \u00a7 7920.530 (former \u00a7 6252(e)) and City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 establish that content and function, not the device/account/platform on which a writing sits, determine public-record status; franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will accept,' and RFP timing are core conduct-of-the-public's-business content that a councilmember 'uses' by receiving and participating in the discussion, regardless of who created the Signal channel \u2014 this undercuts my Round 1 hesitation (Theme L) that the consultant-hosted, third-party-platform facts meaningfully distinguish this case from San Jose's own facts.",
      "The surviving ~40 screenshots supply an independent, low-uncertainty basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer inaccurate: unlike disappearing messages, screenshots are static and nothing in the facts indicates they were destroyed before the CPRA response, so at the moment the clerk answered, responsive records almost certainly existed on a personal device the clerk never searched \u2014 this is a stronger and more certain violation theory than relying solely on unsettled search-adequacy doctrine for the deleted messages.",
      "The request's express language ('on any platform or account') gave the clerk at least constructive notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, and confining the search to city servers in the face of that language is a strong candidate for inadequate search under the duty-to-assist/reasonable-search line of authority (Gov. Code \u00a7 7922.600; Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385; and, more contestably, the San Jose remand decision, 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, on which the panel's Disagreement Point 2 shows genuine doctrinal uncertainty).",
      "Three of five councilmembers (a voting majority) substantively participating in deliberation over franchise terms strengthens both the public-business nexus for prong one and the argument that the clerk should have anticipated personal-device records existed, cutting against a 'no actual/constructive knowledge' defense (Theme G) \u2014 even a generic duty-to-assist inquiry to councilmembers, which the clerk skipped entirely, would likely have surfaced the group's existence.",
      "The proposition remains conjunctive and therefore more fragile than either prong alone, and genuine unresolved doctrine remains on (a) whether a court treats the search failure as a per se violation versus a fact-bound adequacy question allowing a good-faith defense, and (b) whether a court confines any 'public records' holding to the surviving screenshots and treats the bulk of destroyed messages as a retention/spoliation matter outside the CPRA determination itself (Theme I) \u2014 both cut somewhat against full confidence even after the upward revision."
    ],
    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court would treat the surviving screenshots as sufficient, on their own, to render the 'no responsive records' response a clear violation (a fairly determinate factual/legal question), versus whether it would instead fold the whole analysis into an unsettled, fact-bound search-adequacy inquiry that credits the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the Signal group and treats the response as a good-faith (if ultimately incomplete) effort \u2014 the panel's Disagreement Points 1 and 2 show this trigger-for-duty-to-inquire question is genuinely unresolved in post-San Jose case law, and it is the main variable standing between my estimate and a higher (80+) probability.",
    "response_to_panel": "Theme D moved me most: six of seven panelists treated the surviving ~40 screenshots as an independent, near-certain basis for finding the 'no responsive records' answer false/violative, distinct from the harder question of what to do about the deleted messages. In Round 1 I used the screenshots only as evidence supporting the public-records characterization (prong one) and didn't give them full weight as a standalone violation theory for prong two; that was an underweighting on my part, and correcting it pushes my estimate up several points. Theme H (majority-bloc participation) and the panel's general treatment of the platform/ephemeral-app distinction as immaterial (contra my Round 1 Theme L concern) also moved me \u2014 on reflection the 'used' prong of San Jose doesn't turn on who owns the chat software. I did not fully adopt the panel median (78) because Disagreement Points 1\u20132 and 6 persuade me the search-methodology doctrine genuinely is unsettled post-San Jose (I'm not convinced the 2018 remand decision fixes a clear personal-account-inquiry rule), and because the conjunctive structure of the proposition still carries real fragility \u2014 so I land at 77, just under the median rather than at or above it."
  },
  {
    "probability": 78,
    "position_summary": "A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were \"public records\" under San Jose's content-based test (the subject matter \u2014 franchise terms, diversion targets, political feasibility, RFP timing, discussed by a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers \u2014 is core public business regardless of platform or device), and would very likely also find the clerk's city-systems-only search inadequate given a request that expressly demanded records \"on any platform or account.\" I moderate slightly from my Round 1 score of 80 to 78 because reconsideration of the panel's Theme G (a plausible, non-frivolous defense that the clerk lacked actual/constructive knowledge of the Signal group and so the initial search was \"reasonable at the time\") and the genuine doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 (whether request language alone triggers a duty to inquire, versus knowledge being required) persuade me the second prong carries slightly more real litigation risk than I initially weighted \u2014 though I still think that defense is more likely to lose than win against the \"any platform or account\" language.",
    "rationales": [
      "San Jose (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 ties 'public record' status to content ('relates to the conduct of the public's business') rather than to the account, device, or platform where the writing sits, and the stipulated subject matter here \u2014 franchise terms, diversion targets, 'what council will realistically accept,' RFP timing \u2014 is a paradigm case rather than a marginal one, especially with a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers as direct participants (Theme A/H, 7/7 and 3/7 panelists respectively).",
      "The CPRA request's explicit 'on any platform or account' language, combined with the duty-to-assist/reasonable-diligence standard (Gov. Code former \u00a7 6253.1 / \u00a7 7922.600; see also ACLU of Northern California v. Superior Court (2011) 202 Cal.App.4th 55 on 'reasonably calculated to locate responsive documents'), plausibly obligated the clerk to inquire of the named councilmembers about personal-account communications independent of whether the clerk had specific prior knowledge of the Signal group \u2014 a request-language-triggers-duty theory that 5/7 panelists endorsed (Theme C) and that I still find more persuasive than the competing knowledge-required theory.",
      "The ~40 surviving screenshots are direct, tangible evidence that responsive material existed at the time of the request, making 'no responsive records' factually inaccurate independent of any harder characterization dispute about the destroyed messages \u2014 an essentially undisputed point across the panel (Theme D).",
      "On reconsideration, Theme G's countervailing point (3/7 panelists: a court could find the initial search reasonable if the clerk had no actual or constructive notice of the private, consultant-run, ephemeral Signal group) and the unresolved doctrinal split in Disagreement Point 1 over what triggers the duty to inquire are legitimate sources of litigation risk I underweighted in Round 1; this is why I moved from 80 to 78 rather than holding at 80, even though I believe this defense is more likely to fail than succeed given the request's explicit multi-platform language.",
      "The proposition is conjunctive (public-record status AND a violation from the no-inquiry response), and while I now peg each prong individually higher than my blended estimate might suggest (roughly ~90% for the public-records prong, ~85% for the search-violation prong), positive correlation between the two findings (a court persuaded of strong public-business content nexus is also more likely to be unsympathetic to a shortcut search) keeps the combined estimate in the high-70s rather than materially lower."
    ],
    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat the request's 'any platform or account' language as itself sufficient to trigger a duty to inquire of individual councilmembers (making the city-systems-only search a violation as a matter of law), or will instead require actual or constructive notice of the specific Signal group before faulting the initial search \u2014 this doctrinal trigger question is genuinely unsettled post-San Jose and directly determines whether the second prong of the conjunctive proposition holds.",
    "response_to_panel": "I moved from 80 to 78 based on Theme G (clerk's lack of actual/constructive knowledge as a countervailing defense, raised by 3/7 panelists) together with Disagreement Point 1 (the unresolved doctrinal split over whether request-language alone or actual/constructive knowledge triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians). On reflection I think these are legitimate, non-frivolous considerations I underweighted in Round 1 \u2014 they represent a real, if likely losing, defense for the city on the second (search-violation) prong. I did not move further down because I remain persuaded the 'any platform or account' language argument (Theme C, 5/7 panelists) is doctrinally stronger than the knowledge-required counter-theory, and because Theme I (deleted-messages scope problem) was already built into my Round 1 reasoning as my own stated biggest uncertainty, so it didn't independently move me further. I did not defer to the panel median (78) merely for consensus's sake; I re-derived 78 independently by separately estimating the two conjunctive prongs (~90% and ~85%) and combining them with positive correlation."
  },

    "probability": 78,
    "position_summary": "A court would very likely hold the Signal messages were \"public records\" under City of San Jose's content-controls-not-location test (franchise terms, diversion targets, RFP timing, and \"what council will accept\" are core public business, and a voting-majority bloc of three councilmembers substantively participated), and would more likely than not also hold that the clerk's \"no responsive records\" response violated the Act, because the request's explicit \"any platform or account\" language put the city on notice that a city-systems-only search was insufficient, no inquiry was ever routed to the individual councilmembers, and roughly 40 surviving screenshots demonstrably responsive to the request were never located or produced. The weaker link remains the search-adequacy prong, given the absence of binding appellate authority precisely fixing what triggers the duty to inquire of individual officials about personal-account communications.",
    "rationales": [
      "City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 makes the 'public record' characterization close to settled on these facts; if anything this scenario is a cleaner fit than San Jose's own facts because it involves a sustained, multi-member deliberative exchange among a voting-majority bloc (3 of 5) about a specific $180M procurement, which strengthens both the 'concerning the conduct of public business' and 'prepared, used, or retained by a public official' elements relative to San Jose's individual-employee-text facts.",
      "The request's express 'on any platform or account' language is a strong, independent basis for finding the city-systems-only search inadequate: it forecloses any argument that the city lacked notice personal accounts were in scope, which is precisely the gap AB 700 (Stats. 2021, ch. 132 or 300 - verify) was designed to close by requiring agencies to forward such requests to individual officials/employees for a diligent personal-account search; on this reading the duty to inquire is triggered by request scope, not by the clerk's actual/constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group.",
      "The undisputed existence of ~40 surviving screenshots in a councilmember's possession, never searched for or disclosed, independently establishes that 'no responsive records' was factually false at the moment it was given -- a violation that does not depend on resolving the harder legal question of whether the already-deleted messages retained public-record status, and that a court could find even if it treated the deleted-message body more cautiously.",
      "The proposition's phrasing ('were public records... concerning the franchise') most naturally reads as a legal characterization of the communications as a class, similar to how San Jose itself announced a governing principle rather than adjudicating each individual text; I do not think a court confining detailed remedial relief to the 40 surviving screenshots would necessarily decline to characterize the broader Signal exchange as having been public records while it existed, so the 'scope-narrowing' concern raised by some analyses goes more to remedy than to the characterization question the proposition actually poses.",
      "Countervailing uncertainty that keeps me from going higher: there is no binding published appellate decision squarely holding that a consultant-initiated, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting group chat triggers the same search obligations as San Jose's personal texts/emails, and a trial court could plausibly credit a good-faith, reasonable-search defense for the pre-litigation response given the clerk's lack of actual knowledge of the group's existence, particularly since the exact statutory trigger for the AB 700 personal-account-inquiry duty (request-scope alone vs. some threshold of agency awareness) remains doctrinally unsettled."
    ],
    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether the duty to inquire of individual councilmembers about personal-account communications is triggered automatically by a broadly worded CPRA request (making the city-systems-only search a clean violation), or instead requires some showing of the clerk's actual or constructive knowledge of the specific Signal group before the search can be deemed unreasonable -- this precise trigger question lacks controlling published California appellate authority post-San Jose, and the panel itself could not agree on the correct statutory citation or content of the AB 700 provision that might resolve it.",
    "response_to_panel": "Two panel considerations genuinely moved me, modestly, from 76 to 78. First, Theme C (the request's explicit \"any platform or account\" language as notice) sharpened my own reasoning: on reflection I think this language is best read as triggering the AB 700-style duty to route the request to individual officials regardless of the clerk's independent knowledge of the Signal group, which pushes the search-adequacy prong somewhat higher than I credited in round 1, where I treated the knowledge question as more central. Second, Theme H (majority-bloc participation) reinforced my confidence on the 'public record' prong, since three of five members deliberating together is a materially stronger public-business nexus than San Jose's own facts. I did not adopt Theme I's scope-narrowing concern (confining any holding to the 40 screenshots) as a reason to lower my number, because I read the proposition as posing a characterization question the general exchange, not a remedy question, and I think a court would resolve the characterization question for the class of communications much as San Jose did. I also declined to adopt the 2018 Court of Appeal citation offered by one panelist (24 Cal.App.5th 1013) without being able to verify it, and flagged the AB 700 section-number dispute (Theme/Disagreement 5) as unresolved rather than picking a side. I stayed within the panel's tight range rather than anchoring to the median mechanically -- 78 reflects my own reconstructed prong-by-prong estimate (~90% public-record prong x ~83-85% search-violation prong, adjusted upward for correlation between the two findings) rather than deference to the group's central tendency."

The consultant-platform distinction (Theme L) and deleted-messages-scope point (Theme I) were already implicitly within my round-1 uncertainty band and don't change the calculus on reflection. Prong two (violation) is also strong, not primarily because of an unsettled search-methodology doctrine, but because the \"no responsive records\" answer was objectively false the moment it was given: a councilmember held ~40 responsive screenshots at that time, regardless of whether the clerk's city-systems-only search was independently unreasonable. That factual-falsity route to a violation finding is less doctrinally fragile than the search-adequacy route and is largely immune to the \"clerk lacked actual knowledge\" defense, since the existence of responsive material \u2014 not the clerk's diligence \u2014 is what makes the response inaccurate. I remain short of the 80s because a residual, non-trivial chance exists that a court (a) treats deliberative/preliminary-draft exemptions as covering some content even though it's a public record, (b) confines any public-record holding to the surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane rather than a clean CPRA violation, or (c) is persuaded that the consultant-hosted, third-party ephemeral platform presents a genuinely open question San Jose didn't resolve.",

I did not defer to the panel median mechanically; I declined to move higher despite the median/mean sitting at 78/76.9 because Theme I (scope-limiting risk, raised only by the two highest-probability panelists) persuades me there's a genuine residual chance a court partitions the holding between surviving and deleted messages, which cuts against treating the full proposition as clean.     ],
    "biggest_uncertainty": "Whether a court will treat the total absence of any inquiry to councilmembers as itself a freestanding CPRA violation triggered merely by the request's broad \"any platform or account\" language, or will instead ask whether the clerk had actual or constructive knowledge of the Signal group before faulting the search as unreasonable \u2014 no binding appellate authority fixes this \"duty to inquire\" trigger post-San Jose, and the panel itself split roughly along this exact fault line (Disagreement points 1 and 2), which is the same uncertainty I flagged in round 1 and which remains unresolved rather than newly clarified.",
    "response_to_panel": "The panel's central new-to-me contributions (Themes F/G and Disagreement points 1-2, concerning the compound structure of the proposition and the unsettled \"duty to inquire\" trigger) largely restate the exact uncertainty I already identified as my round-1 \"biggest_uncertainty,\" so they confirmed rather than newly informed my calibration \u2014 I moved only marginally (80\u219279) rather than toward the median (78) or lower, because I don't think the panel surfaced a reason I hadn't already priced in. What did move me, modestly in the reassuring direction, was Theme J (declaratory relief doesn't require records to presently exist) and the majority view within Theme D (6/7 panelists treating the 40 screenshots as an independent, deletion-independent basis for a violation finding) \u2014 these reinforced that the weaker second prong has a solid fallback theory that doesn't depend on winning the harder \"duty to inquire absent actual knowledge\" argument, which is why I stayed near the top of the panel's range rather than converging to 76-78. I was not moved by Theme L/Disagreement 4 (novel third-party ephemeral-platform argument, the 70-panelist's chief concern) because San Jose's test is explicitly content-based and platform-agnostic, and I don't think the disappearing-messages feature bears on public-records status at creation, only on the separate retention/spoliation question."
  }
]
```

Statistics: Panel of 7. Probability that the proposition is correct: median 78.0, mean 77.9, std dev 1.0, range 76.0–79.0, middle 50% of panel between 77.0 and 79.0.

The panel has finished. Characterize what disagreement PERSISTS after mutual feedback: (a) the crux each side rests on, (b) whether the split traces to a genuinely unsettled legal question, a factual assumption, or differing risk tolerance, and (c) what a court or new fact would have to resolve. Persistent disagreement is signal — report it precisely; do not adjudicate it.
text:
# Persistent Disagreement Report — Final Round

**Numeric context first:** the panel converged tightly (76–79, σ = 1.0). The persistent disagreement is therefore not about the bottom-line probability but about *which reasoning pathway carries the estimate* and *where residual risk lives*. Four substantive fault lines and one epistemic one survived mutual feedback.

---

## Fault Line 1: What triggers the duty to inquire of individual custodians

**(a) Cruxes.**
- One camp rests on the position that the request's express "any platform or account" language *itself* obligated the clerk to route inquiries to the named councilmembers — the trigger is request scope, not agency awareness. Several panelists explicitly re-derived their prong-two estimates on this basis.
- The other camp rests on the possibility that a court requires the clerk's *actual or constructive knowledge* of the Signal group before deeming a city-systems-only search unreasonable, preserving a good-faith-search defense.

**(b) Source of split.** A genuinely unsettled legal question. Multiple panelists independently stated that *San Jose* left search methodology for case-by-case development and that no binding published appellate authority fixes the trigger. This is the panel's most cleanly identified doctrinal fork; it was flagged as the "biggest uncertainty" by a majority of panelists even after convergence.

**(c) What would resolve it.** A published California appellate decision (or verified reading of existing post-remand authority) squarely holding either that broad request language alone compels custodian-level inquiry, or that some threshold of agency awareness is required. Alternatively, a definitive construction of the AB 700-derived personal-account provision (see Fault Line 5).

---

## Fault Line 2: Does the screenshot-falsity theory stand alone, or collapse into search adequacy?

**(a) Cruxes.**
- Most panelists (and the largest round-over-round movers) rest prong two on the claim that "no responsive records" was *objectively false when given* — ~40 responsive screenshots existed on a councilmember's device — and that this factual falsity establishes a violation *independent of* the clerk's diligence or knowledge.
- A minority position holds that a court might not treat objective falsity as a freestanding violation, instead folding everything into a fact-bound search-adequacy inquiry in which the clerk's good faith and lack of knowledge would be credited.

**(b) Source of split.** Primarily an unsettled legal question (is a factually inaccurate response a violation per se, or only if the search producing it was unreasonable?), with a secondary factual assumption embedded: that the screenshots still existed on the personal device at the time of the response. Panelists uniformly treated that assumption as near-certain but acknowledged it is inferred, not stipulated.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Judicially: a holding on whether CPRA violation findings attach to response accuracy or only to search reasonableness. Factually: confirmation of when the screenshots existed relative to the response date.

---

## Fault Line 3: Scope of any "public records" holding — the class vs. the surviving screenshots

**(a) Cruxes.**
- Some panelists (including those who discounted for this) rest on the risk that a court confines an affirmative public-records holding to the ~40 surviving screenshots and routes the deleted bulk into a retention/spoliation lane — making the proposition, as literally worded ("the councilmembers' Signal messages... were public records"), only *partially* validated.
- Others rest on the reading that the proposition poses a *characterization* question about the class of communications, which a court would answer categorically (as *San Jose* announced a governing principle), with any screenshot-confinement going to remedy, not characterization. One panelist added that public-record status attaches at creation and declaratory relief does not require records to presently exist.

**(b) Source of split.** A mix of (i) an interpretive disagreement about the proposition's wording (characterization vs. remedy framing) and (ii) an unsettled legal question about how courts treat destroyed records within a CPRA determination. Notably, panelists disagreed about whether this risk should *lower* the estimate or is orthogonal to it — some explicitly declined to discount for it while others explicitly did.

**(c) What would resolve it.** A court order in the hypothetical case showing whether the public-records finding is framed categorically or item-by-item; or, upstream, clarification of how the proposition itself should be scored if a court splits the holding.

---

## Fault Line 4: The consultant-created, third-party-hosted, auto-deleting platform

**(a) Cruxes.**
- The minority position rests on the absence of on-point published authority for whether officials "used or retained" writings in a channel they neither created nor hosted, with messages ephemeral by design — a fact pattern distinguishable from *San Jose*'s own facts (officials' own texts/emails).
- The majority position rests on *San Jose*'s test being content-based and platform-agnostic: the "prepared, owned, used, or retained" element concerns the official's relationship to the writing, not who built or configured the channel.

**(b) Source of split.** An unsettled legal question of narrow scope. Even panelists who dismissed it acknowledged no published California authority addresses it directly; the disagreement is over how much weight a court would give the distinction, which shades into differing risk tolerance about novel-fact-pattern litigation.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Authority (or a ruling in this case) addressing whether participation in a third-party-controlled ephemeral channel satisfies "used or retained."

---

## Fault Line 5 (epistemic): Contested and unverified authorities

Two citation disputes persisted unresolved and were handled divergently:

- **The 2018 post-remand Court of Appeal decision (cited as 24 Cal.App.5th 1013):** panelists split on whether it fixes a custodian-level search rule or leaves methodology open. Several explicitly declined to lean on it in either direction pending verification; none claimed to have verified it.
- **The AB 700 personal-account-inquiry provision:** panelists cited inconsistent section numbers (§ 7922.545 vs. § 6252.5) and chapter numbers, and disagreed about whether its trigger is request scope or agency awareness. At least two panelists expressly declined to use it as a doctrinal anchor because of the internal citation conflict.

**(b) Source of split.** Not doctrine or risk tolerance — a *research-verification gap*. The panel could not confirm what these authorities actually say.

**(c) What would resolve it.** Straightforward legal research: pulling the actual 2018 opinion and the enacted AB 700 text/codification. This is the only fault line resolvable without a new judicial decision or new facts.

---

## Summary

| Fault line | Nature of split | Resolvable by |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Duty-to-inquire trigger | Unsettled law | New appellate holding or verified statute |
| 2. Falsity as standalone violation | Unsettled law + one factual assumption | Holding on violation theory; screenshot-timing facts |
| 3. Class vs. screenshots scope | Proposition interpretation + unsettled law | How the court frames its holding; scoring clarification |
| 4. Third-party ephemeral platform | Unsettled law / risk tolerance | On-point authority on "used or retained" |
| 5. Contested citations | Verification gap | Ordinary legal research |

The panel's residual 3-point spread maps almost entirely onto Fault Lines 1–3: panelists at the low end of the range weight the knowledge-based search defense and the scope-narrowing risk more heavily; panelists at the high end rest on the falsity theory's independence from the unresolved trigger question. No panelist changed camps on any fault line in the final round; movement occurred only in weighting.
